summary: DHH reflects on why he loves watches, framing them not as productivity tools but as memento mori—reminders of mortality that encourage spending time well. He draws on Stoicism and existentialism to argue that life's finite constraint is what gives it meaning, rejecting the 'time equals money' mentality as spiritually bankrupt. He hopes immortality is never discovered, believing the hundred-year limit is part of what makes life worthwhile. The post pivots to a lighthearted appreciation of a cheap Casio digital watch, celebrating its nostalgic design and practical simplicity as a perfect daily mortality reminder.
key_points: Watches serve as personal memento mori devices, reminding the wearer of mortality rather than productivity
The 'TIME = MONEY' productivity mindset is spiritually bankrupt—spending time well means living with satisfaction, not maximizing returns
Life's finite constraint gives it meaning; immortality would diminish rather than enhance existence
Stoicism and existentialism converge on the idea that embracing life's shortness is both morbid and liberating
A $20 Casio can be as meaningful and interesting as a mechanical watch when viewed through the right philosophical lens
money_quotes: I really like watches. Not so much because I need to precisely tell time all that often – most of my days, the calendar is pretty empty – but because they remind me that I'm going to die.
The notion that TIME = MONEY – squandered unless invested with a great return! – is spiritually bankrupt.
Life is long enough if you spend it well, but spending it well requires embracing life's shortness.
So much of what we fool ourselves into obsession over is trivial. But also that we could spend our time on things that are not trivial.
It's for the same reason I hope we never do discover immortality. A hundred years, give or take a decade or two, is enough. The constraint is part of what gives the duration its meaning.
What a lovely way to count down to the last day.
key_insight: The awareness of mortality isn't morbid but liberating—it's the finite constraint of life that gives time its meaning and frees us from trivial obsessions.
